does: This paper introduces EACO, a method for aligning multimodal large language models through self-generated preference data without expensive human or GPT-4V annotations. The approach trains a lightweight Critic model on a Scoring Evaluation Instruction-tuning dataset to assess model outputs across multiple dimensions, automatically generating preferred and non-preferred response pairs from the model's own generations. These pairs then feed into Direct Preference Optimization tuning, followed by additional supervised fine-tuning to further boost performance. The method achieves alignment improvements using only 5,000 images, significantly reducing the annotation burden compared to existing preference-learning approaches while maintaining competitive performance on visual question answering and reasoning benchmarks.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in preference-based alignment for language models, extending DPO and similar methods from text-only settings to multimodal models. It responds to the practical bottleneck identified in recent MLLM research where hallucinations and misalignment persist despite instruction fine-tuning, and where preference learning typically requires expensive human annotations or proprietary models like GPT-4V. The paper combines ideas from self-improvement methods in LLMs with the Critic-based evaluation approach, echoing reward modeling and self-critiquing techniques. It addresses open questions about whether alignment can be achieved with minimal external supervision by leveraging the model's own generations and internal assessment capabilities.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more accessible and scalable alignment of multimodal models, particularly valuable for researchers and organizations without access to large annotation budgets or proprietary models. The lightweight Critic framework could be adapted to other vision-language tasks and potentially extended to other modalities. Future work might explore whether Critic-based self-generation generalizes to even smaller datasets, how the method scales to larger models or more diverse visual domains, and whether the approach could enable continual or online alignment without repeated full retraining. The technique potentially facilitates research into preference learning mechanisms in multimodal settings and could inform more efficient fine-tuning strategies for resource-constrained environments.
